record_office_terminology.land_records: County Clerk (deeds, liens, plat maps -- one office per county; Texas General Land Office for original land grants and patents)
record_office_terminology.probate: County Clerk (probate records, wills, estate inventories, guardianships; some counties have a statutory probate court for larger counties)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: County Clerk (marriage licenses from county formation; state registration from 1966)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1903 (births/deaths state registration began; compliance was poor until ~1920; earlier records from county clerks, church records, and cemetery records)

burned_county_authority: Texas State Library and Archives Commission (TSLAC), Austin -- TSLAC holds backup copies of many county records, especially land grants. Texas General Land Office (GLO) has land records independent of county records. FamilySearch microfilm from county courthouses provides additional backups.
administrative_notes: Texas has 254 counties, the most of any US state. The Republic of Texas (1836-1845) was an independent nation before joining the United States. Unlike other states, Texas retained ownership of its public lands at statehood -- the Texas General Land Office (GLO) manages these records, making it uniquely important. Texas was a slave state that seceded and joined the Confederacy. Juneteenth (June 19, 1865) -- when enslaved persons in Texas learned of emancipation -- originated in Galveston. The County Clerk is the primary record office in Texas, handling deeds, marriages, vital records, probate, and court records. Spanish colonial records (1680s-1821) exist for areas in South Texas, the El Paso region, and East Texas (Nacogdoches). Mexican-era records (1821-1836) include empresario colony grants (Stephen F. Austin, Green DeWitt, Martin de Leon, and others). The Texas State Library and Archives Commission (TSLAC) in Austin is the central state repository. Texas has significant German (Hill Country -- Fredericksburg, New Braunfels, Comfort), Czech (Central Texas), Polish (Panna Maria in Karnes County), and Irish (San Patricio colony) immigrant heritage. The Bexar Archives at UT-Austin hold Spanish and Mexican-era records for the San Antonio region. Many Panhandle and West Texas counties were not organized until the 1880s-1930s despite being created on paper in 1876.

core_repositories.source_name: Texas State Library and Archives Commission (TSLAC)
core_repositories.description: Central state archives holding Republic of Texas records, state government records, county records on microfilm, vital records, Confederate pension applications, Republic of Texas claims, Adjutant General records, and the Texas newspapers collection. The genealogy collection includes county histories, family files, and state census-like records. Essential first stop for all Texas genealogical research.

Uniquely important because Texas kept its public lands at statehood. GLO holds all original land grants, patents, surveys, field notes, and maps from the Spanish colonial period through statehood. Empresario colony records (Austin, DeWitt, etc.). Online searchable archives include land grants, maps, and Spanish/Mexican records. The most important land records repository in Texas.

core_repositories.source_name: Dolph Briscoe Center for American History, UT-Austin
core_repositories.repository: Dolph Briscoe Center for American History, University of Texas at Austin
core_repositories.description: Major manuscript repository holding the Bexar Archives (1717-1836, essential for Spanish/Mexican-era San Antonio research), Natchez Trace collection, Texas newspaper archives, ranch records, political papers, and extensive manuscript collections documenting Texas history and families.

familysearch_collections.description: Extensive indexed and browsable collections including Texas county records (deeds, probate, marriages), vital records, church records, and cemetery records. FamilySearch microfilmed extensively at Texas county courthouses and TSLAC. Key collections include Texas marriages, probate records, county tax rolls, and Freedmen's Bureau records.

Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability guides, and links to digitized microfilm. Covers Texas's unique record types (Republic of Texas records, GLO land grants, empresario colonies) and provides research strategies.

newspapers_and_periodicals.source_name: The Portal to Texas History -- UNT Digital Libraries
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Massive digital repository with over 1 million pages of Texas newspapers, maps, photographs, county records, and documents from hundreds of Texas repositories. Searchable by county. The single most important online resource for Texas historical documents.
Digitized historic Texas newspapers. Legal notices, obituaries, estate advertisements, and community news.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Texas Slavery and Freedmen's Bureau Records
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Texas was a major slaveholding state. TSLAC holds county tax rolls listing enslaved persons by slaveholder, estate inventories naming enslaved individuals, and manumission records. NARA Fort Worth holds Texas Freedmen's Bureau records (1865-1872) — labor contracts, marriage records, school records, and complaints. Juneteenth (June 19, 1865) originated in Galveston. East Texas had the heaviest concentration of slaveholding.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Texas Native American Records -- NARA / Oklahoma Historical Society
native_american_and_removal_records.repository: NARA, Fort Worth / Oklahoma Historical Society, Oklahoma City
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Texas had Comanche, Kiowa, Apache, Caddo, Tonkawa, Wichita, and Lipan Apache populations. Most tribes were removed to Indian Territory (Oklahoma) by the 1870s. NARA Fort Worth holds Indian Affairs records. Oklahoma Historical Society has records of tribes relocated from Texas. Meusebach-Comanche Treaty (1847) records at TSLAC. Fort Sill records for Comanche and Kiowa at NARA. Alabama-Coushatta Reservation in Polk County is the only current Texas reservation.

immigration_and_naturalization.repository: TSLAC / NARA Fort Worth / Galveston Immigration Database
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Galveston was a major immigration port (second to Ellis Island for some periods). The Galveston Movement (1907-1914) brought Jewish immigrants. German immigration through Indianola and Galveston (1840s-1880s). Czech, Polish, and Alsatian immigration. Mexican immigration through border crossings (El Paso, Laredo, Brownsville). Naturalization records in county and federal courts. NARA Fort Worth has federal court naturalization records.

military_records.description: Republic of Texas military records at TSLAC. Confederate service records and pension applications (Texas Confederate pension files are among the most detailed). Frontier defense records (Indian Wars, Texas Rangers). NARA holds regular army and volunteer records. Numerous frontier forts (Richardson, Griffin, Davis, Clark, Concho, etc.) have records at NARA. WWI/WWII draft registrations.

military_records.source_name: Civil War Soldiers and Sailors System -- Texas
military_records.description: Searchable database of Civil War service records for Texas Confederate soldiers. Texas contributed approximately 90,000 Confederate troops.

land_and_property.source_name: Texas Land Records -- GLO / County Clerks
land_and_property.repository: Texas General Land Office, Austin / County Clerk offices
land_and_property.description: Texas has a unique land system because it retained its public lands at statehood. GLO holds all original land grants from Spanish, Mexican, Republic, and statehood eras. County Clerks maintain deed books from county formation. GLO online archives are searchable. Headright grants, bounty warrants, and scrip records at GLO. Empresario colony records for Austin's, DeWitt's, and other colonies.

vital_records.source_name: Texas Vital Records -- DSHS / County Clerks
vital_records.repository: Texas Department of State Health Services / County Clerk offices
vital_records.description: State birth and death registration began 1903 but compliance was poor until ~1920. County Clerks maintained vital records locally before state registration. Marriage licenses from county formation. Pre-registration vital events in church records, cemetery records, and newspaper notices. DSHS has state vital records; historical records transferred to TSLAC over time.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Texas Church Records -- Catholic Archives / Local Congregations
church_and_cemetery.repository: Catholic Archives of San Antonio / Local congregations / TSLAC
church_and_cemetery.description: Catholic mission records from the Spanish colonial era (1680s+) are at the Catholic Archives of San Antonio. German Lutheran and Catholic records in Hill Country. Czech Catholic records in Central Texas. Methodist, Baptist, and Presbyterian records from Anglo settlement era. Painted Churches of Texas heritage. African American church records (Baptist, AME) essential for post-emancipation research.

Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Texas. Coverage varies by county. Texas State Cemetery in Austin. Many pioneer cemeteries, family burial grounds, and church cemeteries documented. TxGenWeb cemetery project also active.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide umbrella organization coordinating county and regional genealogical societies across Texas. Publishes Stirpes quarterly journal. Annual conferences and workshops. Links to local societies throughout the state.
Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources for all 254 Texas counties. Cemetery transcriptions, Bible records, query boards, and research guides.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Texas has several extinct or renamed counties and jurisdictions. Under the Republic of Texas (1836-1845), the original counties were created from Mexican municipalities. Davis County (now Cass County) was renamed in 1871. Buchel County (1887-1897) was dissolved back into Brewster and Presidio. Encinal County (1856-1899) was dissolved into Webb and La Salle. Foley County and Greer County (claimed by Texas but awarded to Oklahoma Territory in 1896). Many Panhandle and West Texas counties were created in 1876 but not organized for decades. The old Mexican municipalities (Austin, Brazoria, Goliad, Gonzales, etc.) became the original Texas counties in 1836. Researchers should check Republic of Texas records at TSLAC and GLO for the transitional period.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Texas has 16 burned counties with significant record losses. Courthouse fires were the primary cause of record loss, though the 1900 Galveston hurricane also caused damage. Texas record losses are less severe than Deep South states. TSLAC, GLO, and FamilySearch microfilm provide critical backup sources. Many counties have records reconstructed from private and state sources after fires.

burned_jurisdictions.record_losses: 1900 hurricane caused significant damage to some records
1874 and 1993 courthouse fires; 1993 fire destroyed some records
1877 courthouse fire; some early records lost
